body:
*(Tutor plants the seed for synthesis. No student action yet. References LG2 — the One Thing — and what the student just demonstrated in EC.1. Sets up the closure stem.)*

Tutor narration (student-facing): "You just wrote the same fact two ways with three different rectangles. Now let's go back to the rectangle you started with — the 4-by-5 — and say the One Thing one more time. The equal sign means both sides have the same value. That's true for every fact you write today and every fact you'll write tomorrow."

---

### Connection Task S.1: Both-Forms Closure [GENERALIZATION]

**Purpose:** Lock the One Thing into a short, repeatable statement. Synthesize the arc. No new fact; no new build. The canonical 4 × 5 = 20 rectangle finishes where it started.

**Trigger:** S.1 runs after EC.1 returns Strong (or after the Needs Practice re-pass reaches Strong). If Still Gathering, S.1 is delivered after the Heavy remediation re-pass succeeds.

**Toy:** §2.10 Drop Down + Mode C paired display (canonical 4 × 5 = 20 paired with 20 = 4 × 5 visible above the stem).

**Tutor prompt (student-facing):** "Say it one more time. These two equations are ___." *(Mode C strip displays the canonical 4 × 5 = 20 paired with 20 = 4 × 5 one final time.)*

**Required closure stem:** `I built a 4-by-5 rectangle, so I can write 4 × 5 = 20 AND 20 = 4 × 5 — they say the same thing, and the equal sign means both sides have the same value.`

Drop Down options (student selects to complete "These two equations are ___"): "the same fact written two ways" / "a wrong one and a right one" / "two different facts." Only "the same fact written two ways" is correct.

**Teacher Move:** Post-completion (observable): "You can write it both ways. The equal sign means both sides have the same value — and that's true no matter which way you write it."

**Duration:** 1–2 minutes.

---

header: ### Identity-Building Closure S.2 + M03 Bridge [No Student Action]
body:
*(Tutor closes the synthesis with growth-in-demonstrated-capability framing and explicitly previews M03. No student action; tutor speaks. Per BrainLift DOK3, no fixed-trait identity praise — only observable growth language.)*

Tutor narration (student-facing): "You can now write the same fact two ways. That is a skill you will use in every lesson that comes next. Tomorrow, you'll start finding ALL the rectangles that share the same area — not just one. And every time you check a rectangle, you'll see both ways the equation can be written, just like today. The equal sign means both sides have the same value. Same fact, both ways around. You're ready."

*(Bridge to M03 explicitly names: (a) factor-pair search territory — "finding ALL the rectangles that share the same area" — without using the fenced "factor" / "factor pair" terms in student-facing copy, and (b) Mode C inheritance — "every time you check a rectangle, you'll see both ways." RISK-M02-1 cross-reference: M03 must enforce Mode C in post-Check displays for this bridge to hold.)*

---
